what_manipulated: volitional concealment of personal information
what_measured: judgments of trustworthiness and social valuation of the actor
experiment: Multiple experiments examined whether units choosing to withhold personal information are judged differently by observers than units choosing to reveal the same information. Some studies tested whether observers' judgments vary when the concealment decision is voluntary versus forced. Other studies tested whether concealment actually reduces trustworthiness ratings or only perception thereof. The research tested both unflattering and flattering information domains and compared observer judgments against predictions made by the units themselves.

what_manipulated: exposure to counterstereotypical exemplars; desire for certainty induced via task framing; group membership stereotypes (real or fabricated)
what_measured: evaluation and resource allocation toward counterstereotypical individuals
experiment: Units sorted individuals into groups based on physical appearance and stereotypes. Some units were exposed to counterstereotypical group members and evaluated them, allocating resources. A separate manipulation induced desire for certainty in some units before evaluation. The study tested whether evaluations differed by political ideology and whether increased certainty-seeking in liberals replicated the conservative pattern of negative evaluation.

what_manipulated: information about a target's ecology (desperate vs. hopeful); race information (Black vs. White); combinations of race and ecology cues
what_measured: inferences about the target's behavioral strategy (fast vs. slow life-history traits)
experiment: Units made inferences about individuals based on race cues alone, ecology cues alone, or combinations. Studies initially demonstrated that units hold ecology-based stereotypes. Subsequent studies independently varied race and ecology information to test whether race or ecology dominated inference. Comparisons tested whether the inference pattern tracked race stereotypes or reflected the actual ecology information provided.

what_manipulated: engagement in unethical behavior; delay between behavior and memory assessment
what_measured: clarity and vividness of memory recall for the unethical action versus baseline memory
experiment: Across nine studies with over 2,000 units, participants engaged in unethical actions or control actions and then reported memory of those actions at various time delays. Memory clarity and vividness were measured for unethical versus ethical or neutral actions. The analysis tested whether memory degradation over time was specific to unethical actions and whether this effect predicted repeated unethical behavior.

what_manipulated: teacher mindset training toward empathic discipline (vs. control); exposure to the mindset intervention (online, brief)
what_measured: student suspension rates; student respect for teachers; student classroom motivation
experiment: An initial experiment had teachers adopt either empathic or punitive mindsets and measured the effect on student respect and motivation. A second laboratory study tested whether students maintained motivation under empathic versus punitive discipline. A randomized field experiment exposed teachers in multiple schools to a brief online intervention promoting empathic discipline and measured year-long suspension rates, student respect, and motivation across 31 teachers and 1,682 students.

what_manipulated: whether the cost-benefit calculation is observable to others; time spent deliberating (observable vs. obscured)
what_measured: likelihood of cooperative choice; observer perception of trustworthiness; actual trustworthiness as measured by subsequent behavior
experiment: Two economic game experiments manipulated whether units' decision process was observable. In one, units chose whether to reveal the precise costs of cooperation; in another, time spent deciding was measured. Units' cooperative behavior was compared across observable and non-observable conditions. Separately, observers rated the trustworthiness of units in each condition and participated in subsequent games to measure actual trustworthiness.

what_manipulated: fairness of the allocation procedure (fair vs. unfair); timing of fairness assessment (pre-allocation vs. post-allocation)
what_measured: allocator predictions of receiver happiness; actual receiver happiness; receiver preference for fair vs. unfair procedures
experiment: Allocators assigned resources to receivers using fair or unfair procedures and predicted the happiness impact on receivers. Receivers then reported their actual happiness. Studies tested whether allocators overestimated fairness impact by comparing allocator predictions to receiver reports. Timing manipulations revealed when fairness salience peaks. Final studies directly compared receiver happiness following fair-and-small versus unfair-and-large allocations.

what_manipulated: subjective socioeconomic status relative to others (induced via visual hierarchy ladder or comparative framing)
what_measured: appetite for high-calorie foods; automatic food preference; actual food intake in snack and meal contexts
experiment: Across four studies, units were induced to feel lower, higher, or neutral socioeconomic status relative to peers. Appetite, food preference, and consumption were measured via implicit association, choice tasks, and actual eating opportunities. The studies tested whether status-induced appetite occurred independent of actual financial constraints and whether it generalized across food contexts.

what_manipulated: temporal focus of environmental comparison (past-focused vs. future-focused vs. control); presentation to conservative vs. liberal units
what_measured: climate change attitudes; behavioral intentions regarding climate action; policy support
experiment: Six studies exposed units to information about environmental conditions presented with past or future temporal framings. Responses about climate beliefs, behavioral intentions, and policy preferences were measured. Analyses compared conservative versus liberal units across temporal conditions to test whether past-focus reduced the ideological gap in climate action support.

what_manipulated: presence and salience of thank-you gift incentive in donation request (field and online conditions)
what_measured: donation rate; donation amount; attention to gift versus intrinsic motives
experiment: A field experiment mailed donation requests with and without thank-you gifts to prospective donors. Donation rates and amounts were compared. An online study replicated the effect while manipulating the visual salience of the gift. Analysis tested whether gift effects were driven by attention shift away from intrinsic motives and whether effects were largest for donors with prior high intrinsic motivation.

what_manipulated: facial characteristics of the scientist (competence, morality, attractiveness); presentation format (text, image, video)
what_measured: perceived scientist competence and morality; perceived quality of scientific work; choice to view science communication; judgments of research quality after viewing
experiment: Studies first measured correlations between facial traits and judgments of competence, morality, attractiveness, and interest in the scientist's work. Subsequent studies had units choose which science news items to view or read, paired with scientists varying in appearance. Final studies had units read science news paired with scientists differing in facial characteristics and rate the research quality.

what_manipulated: belief in free will (measured or experimentally weakened via intervention); other units' behavior context (internal vs. external constraint)
what_measured: attribution bias (overestimation of internal factors in others' behavior); punishment and reward recommendations
experiment: Six studies measured the correlation between belief in free will and the correspondence bias in attributions. Two studies experimentally weakened free will belief and measured subsequent attribution changes. A final study tested whether the free will-attribution relationship mediated prescribed punishment and reward behavior.

what_manipulated: role in two-player interaction (thief vs. victim); learning feedback about opponent behavior across rounds
what_measured: strategy choices (rigid vs. flexible punishment/theft); fitness outcomes in the game
experiment: The study combined evolutionary game theory analysis, reinforcement learning simulations, and behavioral experiments with human units playing a theft-punishment game across multiple rounds. Behavior was analyzed to identify whether units relied on flexible adaptive strategies or rigid contingent strategies. Game payoffs measured fitness relative to strategy type and opponent diversity.

what_manipulated: dehumanizing versus humanizing language describing a victim; type of harm (instrumental profit vs. moral justice)
what_measured: support for practices causing harm; willingness to harm victims in vignettes for instrumental gain or moral reasons; spontaneous dehumanization of targets
experiment: Survey studies measured attitudes toward harmful practices (sweatshops, animal testing, drone strikes causing civilians/terrorist deaths) and correlated attributions of reduced humanity with support for harm. Vignette experiments had units decide whether to harm a stranger for money or for their immoral behavior, while manipulating dehumanizing language. Measurement tracked spontaneous dehumanization when imagining harm.

what_manipulated: exposure to information about rising economic inequality (vs. control information); framing of inequality trends
what_measured: beliefs about opportunity (structural vs. individual factors); support for policy addressing inequality
experiment: Units (n=1,501) were shown information about economic inequality trends or control information. They then reported beliefs about the relative importance of structural and individual factors in economic mobility. Separately, they indicated support for various policy approaches to addressing inequality. Analysis tested whether inequality information affected opportunity beliefs and whether this predicted policy support.

what_manipulated: permanence of shared content (temporary vs. permanent storage); task instruction and salience of permanence
what_measured: willingness to take and share self-portraits; disinhibition in photograph content; observer impressions of sharer judgment and character
experiment: Field and online experiments had units take and share self-portraits under temporary or permanent sharing conditions. Compliance with the sharing request and disinhibition in photo content were measured. Separately, observers rated the judgment and character of sharers based on the photos, either knowing the original sharing condition or not. Follow-up studies examined persistence of impressions and effects among friends.

what_manipulated: information search strategy (comparative vs. integrative features); time horizon comparison (sooner vs. later reward framing); unobtrusive search manipulation
what_measured: discounting of delayed rewards; patience in intertemporal choice; susceptibility to acceleration versus delay framing
experiment: Initial observation identified heterogeneity in search strategies during choice between sooner and later rewards. A manipulation randomly assigned units to conditions promoting comparative (feature-by-feature) or integrative (option-by-option) search. Comparisons measured how choice and discounting rates differed by strategy and whether search effects persisted when participants were unaware of the manipulation.

what_manipulated: exposure to information about past racial economic equality; salience of societal discrimination; anchoring on personal circumstances; demographic groups (White, Black; high-income, low-income)
what_measured: perceived Black-White economic equality (past and current); accuracy of estimates versus national statistics
experiment: A survey (n=1,377) measured estimates of Black-White economic equality across income and race groups, comparing estimates to actual statistics. Follow-up experiments tested whether making discrimination salient improved estimate accuracy and whether personal anchoring increased overestimation. Analysis examined whether optimism bias and belief in a just world predicted overestimates.

what_manipulated: training in cognitive regulation strategies for food craving (versus control); emphasis on negative consequences of unhealthy foods versus positive benefits of healthy foods; brief training exposure (ROC-T)
what_measured: food craving for healthy versus unhealthy items; food valuation (willingness to pay); food choice distribution; actual food consumption
experiment: Studies first showed that cognitive strategies modified craving for both unhealthy and healthy foods and changed valuation. Subsequent studies tested a brief training program (Regulation of Craving Training) on food choices in laboratory and applied settings. Measurements included choice shifts to healthier options, generalization to nontrained items, and actual caloric intake reduction. A final study confirmed training was necessary beyond framing effects alone.

what_manipulated: exposure to opposing beliefs in a bipartisan social network (vs. single-party or control); partisan priming (party logos or peer identities); climate communication from NASA
what_measured: interpretation accuracy of climate data; belief polarization (conservatives vs. liberals); social learning of target interpretation
experiment: An online experiment exposed units to structured bipartisan social networks discussing climate data, with or without partisan priming. Units' interpretations of climate trends and polarization were measured before and after network exposure. Analysis compared conditions with and without partisan salience and tested whether social learning mechanisms explained improvements in accuracy or maintenance of polarization.
